addedBlocks:
## devops-mcp
This DevOps MCP intentionally exposes a small always-on MCP tool surface:
health, list_capabilities, get_capability_details, tool_search, and invoke_tool.

For every host, Docker, systemd, file, or audit-log task, call tool_search first
to find the right hidden operation. Then call invoke_tool with the exact operation
name and an args object matching the operation description. Do not assume direct
tools such as run_command, docker_ps, read_file, or service_status are listed in
tools/list; they are discoverable operations to keep client context small.

Call list_capabilities to browse by category or safety class. Call
get_capability_details for one operation's full contract, including args and
examples. Call health first when you need server context, visible tools,
registered agents, or the complete operation-name list. Treat write-capable
operations with care: this server has root-level host access, so prefer
inspection commands before state-changing commands.

## recall-ai
Recall.ai lets developers send meeting bots to video calls, capture meeting data, and connect bots to calendar events. This MCP server provides read-only tools scoped to a Recall.ai workspace. A workspace is the Recall.ai account context that bots, calendars, docs defaults, and logs are read from. By default, the MCP uses the workspace selected when the user authorized the connection. If the account has access to multiple workspaces, workspace-scoped tools accept an optional workspace_id so the agent can read from a different workspace for that call. Use get_info to see the current workspace and API version. When the user names a workspace, call list_workspaces, match the name from the returned list, then pass the returned workspace ID to bot, calendar, log, and docs tools. Do not pass a workspace name as workspace_id. Use bot tools to list bots, inspect bot details and lifecycle events, and page through bot logs when debugging join, recording, or meeting behavior. Use list_calendars/get_calendar to inspect connected and disconnected calendar integrations, platform emails, credential status, and calendar connection/sync state. Use calendar event tools to see which bots were scheduled from calendar events. Use log tools for workspace-level API, calendar connection, recording, transcript, webhook, and integration errors when a bot-specific log query is too narrow. Use list_webhook_endpoints and list_webhook_deliveries when debugging dashboard webhook subscriptions, missing delivery attempts, destination responses, or a resource-specific webhook event such as a bot, recording, transcript, or artifact event. Use docs tools to search and fetch Recall.ai guides and API reference material for the current workspace's API version. Use search_docs when attempting to learn docs slugs, unlessthe slug came from list_docs, a Recall guide resource, or an explicit user-provided docs URL/slug. When drafting user-facing answers or understanding bot behavior, use docs tools for product behavior, API defaults, configuration fields, … [truncated]

## synology-monitor
This NAS MCP intentionally exposes only a small always-on tool surface:
list_capabilities, get_capability_details, tool_search, invoke_tool, run_command,
check_disk_space, and restart_nas_api.

For any NAS diagnostic, troubleshooting, recovery, storage, Drive/ShareSync,
backup, package, log, filesystem, snapshot, permission, or admin task, call
tool_search first unless the task is exactly disk-space checking, free-form
read-only shell diagnosis, or restarting nas-api. Most NAS capabilities are
hidden from tools/list by design to keep the AI session context small.

list_capabilities browses by group and safety class without invoking anything.
get_capability_details returns one operation's full contract. tool_search returns
clear operation descriptions plus the exact invoke_tool call shape. After choosing
a capability, call invoke_tool with the exact returned name, target, and args. Use
target "edgesynology1", "edgesynology2", or "both".

Write tools require a preview first. To execute after reviewing the preview,
call invoke_tool again with confirmed: true inside args. Do not invent or expose
bearer tokens.

## trigger
Trigger.dev MCP server to automate your Trigger.dev projects and answer questions about Trigger.dev by searching the docs. 
If you need help setting up Trigger.dev in your project please refer to https://trigger.dev/docs/manual-setup. 
If the user asks for help with adding Trigger.dev to their project, please refer to https://trigger.dev/docs/manual-setup.

## Windows-MCP

Windows MCP server provides tools to interact directly with the Windows desktop,
thus enabling to operate the desktop on the user's behalf.

---head---
# fix_ADAPTER_BUGS.md — Permanent, root-cause fixes for the four model-adapter bugs

**Audience:** a fresh AI engineering session with no memory of how these bugs were found.
**Mandate from the repo owner (Albert):** *"Proper, long-term fix. Never a short-term fix or band-aid. Never accept anything less than perfect, not for now, not ever."* Treat every instruction here as a floor, not a ceiling. If you find a deeper or more correct fix than what is written, take it — but never a narrower or more temporary one.

You must carry this all the way to completion **without asking the owner anything**: fix → prove against live provider APIs → deploy the worker → re-run the full extraction/vision bake-off → set production to the genuinely-best models → commit + push → write the results report. Everything you need is below or discoverable in the repo. Do not stop at "code compiles." Done means "proven correct against the real APIs in production and the bake-off re-run."

---

## 0. Context: what these bugs are and how they were discovered

The Oracle ingests images and documents in two passes:
1. **VISION** — a vision model transcribes an uploaded image to text (`default_vision_route`).
2. **EXTRACTION** — a text model turns that transcript into claims JSON (`default_extraction_route`).

Inference path: `OracleAIClient → ModelRouter → provider adapter`. Approved models live in DB settings pools (`model_pool_extraction`, etc.). When a model fails, the pool advances to the next candidate (`AllCandidatesFailedError` only if *all* fail). **This pool-advancement masked the bugs:** a model that 400s instantly looks "fine" because the chain silently moves on to one that works.

A controlled bake-off (isolating each model into a one-element pool) revealed that **3 of 4 extraction models and 1 of 2 vision models are hard-broken in current production.** The only models that actually run today are extraction=`gemini-3.1-flash-lite` (works but under-extracts — ~7 claims from a ~74-edge diagram) and vision=`qwen3-vl` (verified accurate). Everything else fails before producing a claim. The four root causes are below.

The owner does **not** want the weak-but-working `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` as the permanent extractor. He wants every candidate model genuinely working so the bake-off can pick the best on quality, not on "it was the only one that didn't crash."

**Partial fixes already exist in the working tree** (started by a prior session). Do not assume they are correct or complete — verify each against the acceptance criteria and the live API. Some are wrong (see Bug 3).

---

## 1. THE FOUR BUGS

### Bug 1 — OpenAI strict structured output rejects the extraction schema
**Symptom:** `gpt-4o-mini` and `gpt-4.1-mini` extraction calls 400 instantly with an error about a missing property (e.g. `Missing 'sensitivityReason'`). Both OpenAI extraction models are therefore unusable.

content.content:
1	# fix_ADAPTER_BUGS.md — Permanent, root-cause fixes for the four model-adapter bugs
2	
3	**Audience:** a fresh AI engineering session with no memory of how these bugs were found.
4	**Mandate from the repo owner (Albert):** *"Proper, long-term fix. Never a short-term fix or band-aid. Never accept anything less than perfect, not for now, not ever."* Treat every instruction here as a floor, not a ceiling. If you find a deeper or more correct fix than what is written, take it — but never a narrower or more temporary one.
5	
6	You must carry this all the way to completion **without asking the owner anything**: fix → prove against live provider APIs → deploy the worker → re-run the full extraction/vision bake-off → set production to the genuinely-best models → commit + push → write the results report. Everything you need is below or discoverable in the repo. Do not stop at "code compiles." Done means "proven correct against the real APIs in production and the bake-off re-run."
7	
8	---
9	
10	## 0. Context: what these bugs are and how they were discovered
11	
12	The Oracle ingests images and documents in two passes:
13	1. **VISION** — a vision model transcribes an uploaded image to text (`default_vision_route`).
14	2. **EXTRACTION** — a text model turns that transcript into claims JSON (`default_extraction_route`).
15	
16	Inference path: `OracleAIClient → ModelRouter → provider adapter`. Approved models live in DB settings pools (`model_pool_extraction`, etc.). When a model fails, the pool advances to the next candidate (`AllCandidatesFailedError` only if *all* fail). **This pool-advancement masked the bugs:** a model that 400s instantly looks "fine" because the chain silently moves on to one that works.
17	
18	A controlled bake-off (isolating each model into a one-element pool) revealed that **3 of 4 extraction models and 1 of 2 vision models are hard-broken in current production.** The only models that actually run today are extraction=`gemini-3.1-flash-lite` (works but under-extracts — ~7 claims from a ~74-edge diagram) and vision=`qwen3-vl` (verified accurate). Everything else fails before producing a claim. The four root causes are below.
19	
20	The owner does **not** want the weak-but-working `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` as the permanent extractor. He wants every candidate model genuinely working so the bake-off can pick the best on quality, not on "it was the only one that didn't crash."
21	
22	**Partial fixes already exist in the working tree** (started by a prior session). Do not assume they are correct or complete — verify each against the acceptance criteria and the live API. Some are wrong (see Bug 3).
23	
24	---
25	
26	## 1. THE FOUR BUGS
27	
28	### Bug 1 — OpenAI strict structured output rejects the extraction schema
29	**Symptom:** `gpt-4o-mini` and `gpt-4.1-mini` extraction calls 400 instantly with an error about a missing property (e.g. `Missing 'sensitivityReason'`). Both OpenAI extraction models are therefore unusable.
30	
31	**Root cause:** OpenAI's strict JSON-schema mode (`response_format.json_schema.strict: true`) requires that **every** property appear in the object's `required` array (and `additionalProperties: false`). The Zod→JSON-schema converter only lists non-`.optional()` keys in `required`. The extraction schema has several optional fields (`packages/ai/src/prompts/extraction-system.ts` — `.optional()` at roughly lines 152, 190, 201, 205, 210, 223, including `sensitivityReason` / `sensitivityFlags`). Those optional keys are absent from `required`, so strict mode 400s.
32	
33	**Current partial fix (verify, then make perfect):** `packages/ai/src/providers/openai-adapter.ts` has `stripIncompatibleFields()` → `walk()` (~line 387) that deletes `$schema`/`default` and recomputes `required = Object.keys(properties)` for every object node. It is applied in both `generateObject` (line ~110) and `submitBatch` (line ~174).
34	
35	**Why this is not yet "perfect" and what you must do:**
36	- Forcing a genuinely-optional field into `required` without also making it **nullable** forces the model to always emit a value, which is semantically wrong for fields that are meant to be absent sometimes. The canonical OpenAI-strict pattern is: *every property is in `required`, and formerly-optional properties become nullable* (`type: ["string","null"]`, or a union with `{"type":"null"}`). Implement that in `walk()`: for each property key that was **not** originally in the object's `required` set, union its type with `null` (handle `type` as string, `type` as array, and `anyOf`/`oneOf`/`$ref` shapes). Then recompute `required` to all keys.
37	- Confirm the downstream Zod parse still accepts a `null` for those fields (the schema uses `.optional()`; `null` is not `undefined`). If Zod rejects `null`, either map `.optional()` → `.nullish()` in the schema, or normalize `null`→`undefined` before validation in the OpenAI adapter. Pick whichever keeps a single source of truth and does not weaken validation for the other providers. **Do not** silently coerce or `.catch()` away validation errors (a prior session tried that; it was reverted as a band-aid).
38	- The strip/nullable transform must be **OpenAI-only**. Gemini/Vertex must keep receiving the original schema (they do not require all-keys-required and they reject some OpenAI-isms). Verify you are not changing the shared `zodToJsonSchema` output for the other adapters.
39	
40	**Acceptance:** `gpt-4o-mini` AND `gpt-4.1-mini` complete a real extraction call against the live OpenAI API on the bake-off document and return schema-valid claims (no 400, no refusal). Optional fields that the model legitimately omits come back as `null`/absent and pass Zod.
41	
42	---
43	
44	### Bug 2 — Gemini (Google Developer API) hard 60-second timeout
45	**Symptom:** `google/gemini-2.5-flash` extraction aborts at ~60.1s on dense image-derived transcripts, every time.
46	
47	**Root cause:** a hard-coded `AbortSignal.timeout(60_000)` in the Google Gemini adapter.
48	
49	**Current partial fix (verify):** `packages/ai/src/providers/google-gemini-adapter.ts` now defines `DEFAULT_GEMINI_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS = 180_000`, a `requestTimeoutMs` constructor option, and a `GOOGLE_GEMINI_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS` env override; the SDK client gets `httpOptions: { timeout: this.requestTimeoutMs }` and the OAuth/REST path uses `AbortSignal.timeout(this.requestTimeoutMs)`.
50	
51	**What you must do:** Verify there is **no remaining hard-coded 60s** anywhere in this adapter or any other adapter (grep `60_000`, `60000`, `timeout`). Confirm the timeout applies on **both** code paths (API-key SDK path and service-account OAuth/REST path). Confirm 180s is comfortably above the real p99 for a dense extraction (the bake-off transcripts can take >60s; if you observe calls approaching 180s, raise the default and note it). A timeout must never be the thing that kills a legitimate call.
52	
53	**Acceptance:** `google/gemini-2.5-flash` completes a real extraction on the bake-off document via the Google Developer API without an abort, on whichever auth path production actually uses.
54	
55	---
56	
57	### Bug 3 — Gemini `thinkingLevel` unconditionally attached, breaks gemini-2.5-flash vision (NOT actually fixed yet)
58	**Symptom:** `gemini-2.5-flash` as a **vision** model 400s with "Thinking level not supported" (or equivalent).
59	
60	**Root cause:** `geminiThinkingConfig()` attached a `thinkingConfig.thinkingLevel` to every call.
61	
62	**Current partial fix is INSUFFICIENT — this is the one that is still broken.** `geminiThinkingConfig(route)` now early-returns `{}` when `!route.supportsReasoningControls`. **But the model catalog marks `gemini-2.5-flash` with `supportsReasoningControls: true`** (`packages/ai/src/routes/catalog.ts:49`). So for `gemini-2.5-flash` the guard does nothing — `thinkingLevel` is still attached and vision still 400s. The partial fix only helps models already flagged `false`.
63	
64	**You must determine the true root cause against the live API and fix it properly.** Investigate and decide among (not limited to) these — verify empirically, do not guess:
65	- Whether `gemini-2.5-flash` rejects the **`thinkingLevel` enum** specifically but accepts a numeric **`thinkingBudget`** (the older field). If so, the adapter should emit the field shape the target model actually supports — i.e. capability must encode *how* a model expresses thinking, not just a boolean.
66	- Whether thinking config is simply invalid when the request includes **image input** (vision) for this model, regardless of text support — in which case vision calls must never carry thinking config.
67	- Whether the catalog flag for `gemini-2.5-flash` is itself wrong for the vision use, requiring a more precise capability model (e.g. `thinking` supported for text but the `thinkingLevel` enum unsupported on this model generation).
68

Whatever canonical form you choose (raw text vs. single-JSON), make the **writer, the reader, and every other settings consumer agree** — verify there is not a second writer (e.g. the bake-off `_set` tooling, server actions, or migrations) using a different convention.

Add a regression guard (unit test or a `__verify__` assertion) that fails if a settings value is double-encoded.

89	- Asserts a schema-valid, non-empty result.

93	- **Google Gemini** (`google/gemini-2.5-flash`): Developer API via `GEMINI_API_KEY`/`GOOGLE_API_KEY`, or service-account OAuth via `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS_JSON`. Determine which production uses and test that path.

95	- **Qwen vision** (`qwen3-vl`): DashScope, **international region**. `DASHSCOPE_API_KEY` is set and `DASHSCOPE_BASE_URL` must point at `dashscope-intl` (the model + key live there, not the US default). Already verified working — use as the vision known-good.

# fix_ADAPTER_BUGS.md — Permanent, root-cause fixes for the four model-adapter bugs

**Audience:** a fresh AI engineering session with no memory of how these bugs were found.
**Mandate from the repo owner (Albert):** *"Proper, long-term fix. Never a short-term fix or band-aid. Never accept anything less than perfect, not for now, not ever."* Treat every instruction here as a floor, not a ceiling. If you find a deeper or more correct fix than what is written, take it — but never a narrower or more temporary one.

You must carry this all the way to completion **without asking the owner anything**: fix → prove against live provider APIs → deploy the worker → re-run the full extraction/vision bake-off → set production to the genuinely-best models → commit + push → write the results report. Everything you need is below or discoverable in the repo. Do not stop at "code compiles." Done means "proven correct against the real APIs in production and the bake-off re-run."

---

## 0. Context: what these bugs are and how they were discovered

The Oracle ingests images and documents in two passes:
1. **VISION** — a vision model transcribes an uploaded image to text (`default_vision_route`).
2. **EXTRACTION** — a text model turns that transcript into claims JSON (`default_extraction_route`).

Inference path: `OracleAIClient → ModelRouter → provider adapter`. Approved models live in DB settings pools (`model_pool_extraction`, etc.). When a model fails, the pool advances to the next candidate (`AllCandidatesFailedError` only if *all* fail). **This pool-advancement masked the bugs:** a model that 400s instantly looks "fine" because the chain silently moves on to one that works.

A controlled bake-off (isolating each model into a one-element pool) revealed that **3 of 4 extraction models and 1 of 2 vision models are hard-broken in current production.** The only models that actually run today are extraction=`gemini-3.1-flash-lite` (works but under-extracts — ~7 claims from a ~74-edge diagram) and vision=`qwen3-vl` (verified accurate). Everything else fails before producing a claim. The four root causes are below.

The owner does **not** want the weak-but-working `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` as the permanent extractor. He wants every candidate model genuinely working so the bake-off can pick the best on quality, not on "it was the only one that didn't crash."

**Partial fixes already exist in the working tree** (started by a prior session). Do not assume they are correct or complete — verify each against the acceptance criteria and the live API. Some are wrong (see Bug 3).

---

## 1. THE FOUR BUGS

### Bug 1 — OpenAI strict structured output rejects the extraction schema
**Symptom:** `gpt-4o-mini` and `gpt-4.1-mini` extraction calls 400 instantly with an error about a missing property (e.g. `Missing 'sensitivityReason'`). Both OpenAI extraction models are therefore unusable.

**Root cause:** OpenAI's strict JSON-schema mode (`response_format.json_schema.strict: true`) requires that **every** property appear in the object's `required` array (and `additionalProperties: false`). The Zod→JSON-schema converter only lists non-`.optional()` keys in `required`. The extraction schema has several optional fields (`packages/ai/src/prompts/extraction-system.ts` — `.optional()` at roughly lines 152, 190, 201, 205, 210, 223, including `sensitivityReason` / `sensitivityFlags`). Those optional keys are absent from `required`, so strict mode 400s.

**Current partial fix (verify, then make perfect):** `packages/ai/src/providers/openai-adapter.ts` has `stripIncompatibleFields()` → `walk()` (~line 387) that deletes `$schema`/`default` and recomputes `required = Object.keys(properties)` for every object node. It is applied in both `generateObject` (line ~110) and `submitBatch` (line ~174).

**Why this is not yet "perfect" and what you must do:**
- Forcing a genuinely-optional field into `required` without also making it **nullable** forces the model to always emit a value, which is semantically wrong for fields that are meant to be absent sometimes. The canonical OpenAI-strict pattern is: *every property is in `required`, and formerly-optional properties become nullable* (`type: ["string","null"]`, or a union with `{"type":"null"}`). Implement that in `walk()`: for each property key that was **not** originally in the object's `required` set, union its type with `null` (handle `type` as string, `type` as array, and `anyOf`/`oneOf`/`$ref` shapes). Then recompute `required` to all keys.
- Confirm the downstream Zod parse still accepts a `null` for those fields (the schema uses `.optional()`; `null` is not `undefined`). If Zod rejects `null`, either map `.optional()` → `.nullish()` in the schema, or normalize `null`→`undefined` before validation in the OpenAI adapter. Pick whichever keeps a single source of truth and does not weaken validation for the other providers. **Do not** silently coerce or `.catch()` away validation errors (a prior session tried that; it was reverted as a band-aid).
- The strip/nullable transform must be **OpenAI-only**. Gemini/Vertex must keep receiving the original schema (they do not require all-keys-required and they reject some OpenAI-isms). Verify you are not changing the shared `zodToJsonSchema` output for the other adapters.

**Acceptance:** `gpt-4o-mini` AND `gpt-4.1-mini` complete a real extraction call against the live OpenAI API on the bake-off document and return schema-valid claims (no 400, no refusal). Optional fields that the model legitimately omits come back as `null`/absent and pass Zod.

---

### Bug 2 — Gemini (Google Developer API) hard 60-second timeout
**Symptom:** `google/gemini-2.5-flash` extraction aborts at ~60.1s on dense image-derived transcripts, every time.

**Root cause:** a hard-coded `AbortSignal.timeout(60_000)` in the Google Gemini adapter.

**Current partial fix (verify):** `packages/ai/src/providers/google-gemini-adapter.ts` now defines `DEFAULT_GEMINI_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS = 180_000`, a `requestTimeoutMs` constructor option, and a `GOOGLE_GEMINI_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS` env override; the SDK client gets `httpOptions: { timeout: this.requestTimeoutMs }` and the OAuth/REST path uses `AbortSignal.timeout(this.requestTimeoutMs)`.

**What you must do:** Verify there is **no remaining hard-coded 60s** anywhere in this adapter or any other adapter (grep `60_000`, `60000`, `timeout`). Confirm the timeout applies on **both** code paths (API-key SDK path and service-account OAuth/REST path). Confirm 180s is comfortably above the real p99 for a dense extraction (the bake-off transcripts can take >60s; if you observe calls approaching 180s, raise the default and note it). A timeout must never be the thing that kills a legitimate call.

**Acceptance:** `google/gemini-2.5-flash` completes a real extraction on the bake-off document via the Google Developer API without an abort, on whichever auth path production actually uses.

---

### Bug 3 — Gemini `thinkingLevel` unconditionally attached, breaks gemini-2.5-flash vision (NOT actually fixed yet)
**Symptom:** `gemini-2.5-flash` as a **vision** model 400s with "Thinking level not supported" (or equivalent).

**Root cause:** `geminiThinkingConfig()` attached a `thinkingConfig.thinkingLevel` to every call.

**Current partial fix is INSUFFICIENT — this is the one that is still broken.** `geminiThinkingConfig(route)` now early-returns `{}` when `!route.supportsReasoningControls`. **But the model catalog marks `gemini-2.5-flash` with `supportsReasoningControls: true`** (`packages/ai/src/routes/catalog.ts:49`). So for `gemini-2.5-flash` the guard does nothing — `thinkingLevel` is still attached and vision still 400s. The partial fix only helps models already flagged `false`.

**You must determine the true root cause against the live API and fix it properly.** Investigate and decide among (not limited to) these — verify empirically, do not guess:
- Whether `gemini-2.5-flash` rejects the **`thinkingLevel` enum** specifically but accepts a numeric **`thinkingBudget`** (the older field). If so, the adapter should emit the field shape the target model actually supports — i.e. capability must encode *how* a model expresses thinking, not just a boolean.
- Whether thinking config is simply invalid when the request includes **image input** (vision) for this model, regardless of text support — in which case vision calls must never carry thinking config.
- Whether the catalog flag for `gemini-2.5-flash` is itself wrong for the vision use, requiring a more precise capability model (e.g. `thinking` supported for text but the `thinkingLevel` enum unsupported on this model generation).

The "perfect" fix is a capability model that is correct per-model and per-modality, driven from `catalog.ts`/`candidates.ts` metadata — **not** a special-case `if (modelId === 'gemini-2.5-flash')`. No hard-coded model names in adapter logic (the owner has been explicit and repeatedly insistent: **nothing model-specific may be hard-coded in adapters**; model facts live in the catalog/capability layer and flow through the route).

**Acceptance:** `gemini-2.5-flash` completes a real **vision** transcription of the bake-off image via the live API with no thinking-related 400, while any model that *does* support `thinkingLevel` still receives it for reasoning-bearing roles. Re-verify Bug 2's `google/gemini-2.5-flash` extraction still passes after this change.

---

### Bug 4 — Settings value double-JSON-encoded (data fixed; find & fix the WRITER)
**Symptom:** `default_extraction_reasoning_effort` was stored as a double-encoded string (`"\"off\""` / `"\"google/gemini-2.5-flash\""`-style) in the `settings` table, so consumers read a quoted string instead of the value. The **data** has already been repaired to a single-encoded `"off"` in production. **The code path that produced it has NOT been fixed.**

**Root cause to find:** trace the write path from `apps/web/app/admin/settings/_components/model-picker.tsx` (it `fetch`/POSTs a JSON body at ~lines 267, 280) → the API route handler it calls → the `settings` table write (Drizzle `settings` schema, imported in `apps/web/app/admin/settings/page.tsx`). Somewhere a value that is already a JSON string is `JSON.stringify`'d again (double-encode), or a value that should be stored raw is being stringified when the column/consumer expects the bare value. Find the exact line and fix it so a write is **idempotent**: writing `off` stores `off` (or a single, consistent canonical encoding), and reading it back yields `off`. Whatever canonical form you choose (raw text vs. single-JSON), make the **writer, the reader, and every other settings consumer agree** — verify there is not a second writer (e.g. the bake-off `_set` tooling, server actions, or migrations) using a different convention.

**Acceptance:** Round-trip test: set each model/effort setting through the **real admin write path**, read it back through the **real consumer path** (`page.tsx` loader + the router's settings reader), and confirm no extra quoting at any layer. Add a regression guard (unit test or a `__verify__` assertion) that fails if a settings value is double-encoded.

---

## 2. PROVE IT — no fix counts until it runs against the live APIs

For each bug, write or reuse a small Node/tsx harness under `packages/ai/src/__verify__/` (there is already `r-providers-smoke.ts` you can model on) **or** a throwaway script you delete afterward, that:
- Builds the real route for the model from the catalog.
- Calls the real adapter `generateObject` (extraction) / `generateText`/vision (transcription) against the **live provider API** using the env keys already configured in this workspace.
- Asserts a schema-valid, non-empty result.

Provider/region notes you will need:
- **OpenAI** (`gpt-4o-mini`, `gpt-4.1-mini`): `OPENAI_API_KEY` is set.
- **Google Gemini** (`google/gemini-2.5-flash`): Developer API via `GEMINI_API_KEY`/`GOOGLE_API_KEY`, or service-account OAuth via `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS_JSON`. Determine which production uses and test that path.
- **Vertex Gemini** (`vertex/...` routes): ADC/service-account; separate adapter from Google. (This adapter already works — it produced 56–81 claims on the bake-off doc earlier. Keep it as a known-good comparison point.)
- **Qwen vision** (`qwen3-vl`): DashScope, **international region**. `DASHSCOPE_API_KEY` is set and `DASHSCOPE_BASE_URL` must point at `dashscope-intl` (the model + key live there, not the US default). Already verified working — use as the vision known-good.

If a provider key is genuinely missing for a model you must test, do **not** ask the owner and do **not** skip it silently: report it explicitly in the final results as "could not verify — key X absent," and verify everything else. (Per the workspace credential rule, you may *use* keys already set in the environment, but you must never ask the owner to hand you a secret in chat.)

---

## 3. GATES — run before deploying

From repo root, in order; everything must pass (or be no-worse than the pre-existing baseline you record first):
```
pnpm -w typecheck      # or: pnpm turbo typecheck
pnpm -w lint
pnpm -w build
pnpm --filter @oracle/ai test   # if a test script exists; plus any __verify__ smokes you added
```
Record the baseline (run them once before you change anything) so you can prove you introduced no new failures. Do not weaken or `// @ts-expect-error` your way past a real failure.

---

## 4. DEPLOY THE WORKER

The vision + extraction passes run inside the **Trigger.dev worker** (`apps/workers`, `document-ingestion.ts`), which consumes `packages/ai`. Code fixes do nothing in production until the worker is redeployed.

- Trigger.dev project: `proj_wgpzsvhmsopqhvwqaycn`. Use the Trigger MCP (`mcp__trigger__deploy`) or the project's deploy command.
- Deploy to the environment production ingestion uses (confirm prod vs staging before deploying).
- After deploy, confirm the new worker version is live (`mcp__trigger__get_current_worker` / `list_deploys`).
- **Async runs:** when you trigger ingestion/test runs, **block on completion** (`mcp__trigger__wait_for_run_to_complete`). Do NOT fire a run and then poll once and move on — a prior session repeatedly stalled by single-polling async runs. Wait for terminal state every time.

---

## 5. RE-RUN THE FULL BAKE-OFF (the real comparison, now that models work)

The protocol is already written in **`fix_TESTPLAN.md`** (read it in full). `fix_TESTPLAN_RESULTS.md` holds the prior (blocked) run for reference. Re-run it now that the candidates actually execute:

- **Extraction candidates:** `gpt-4o-mini`, `gemini-2.5-flash` (Google adapter), `gpt-4.1-mini`, plus the known-good comparators `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` and the **Vertex** `gemini-2.5-flash` route (it extracted 56–81 claims earlier — include it; it may be the winner).
- **Vision candidates:** `qwen3-vl` (known-good) vs `gemini-2.5-flash` vision (now that Bug 3 is fixed) — and any cost-appropriate Gemini vision model named in the test plan.
- Use the **same** bake-off document/image as the prior run so results are comparable. Isolate each model into a one-element pool (set the relevant `model_pool_*` / `default_*_route` settings) so pool-advancement cannot mask a failure — exactly how the breakage was originally exposed.
- Capture for every model: claim count, schema-valid vs rejected, cost, latency, and a qualitative read of claim quality (faithfulness to the diagram's edges/lanes — the diagram has known ground truths: e.g. Gina → "Review Audit and send to factory" / white column; Carlos → "SKUs creation" / peach column). Dump the actual claims and transcript to files (keep heavy data in files, not inline) so the comparison is auditable.

**Settings-safety rules while you mutate production settings:**
- Settings values are single-encoded (see Bug 4). Never write a double-encoded value. After every write, read back and confirm the encoding.
- Before you start, record the current production settings (route ids, pools, `enforce_model_capabilities`) verbatim so you can restore exactly.
- If anything goes wrong or you must abort, restore the known-good safe pair: extraction=`gemini-3.1-flash-lite`, vision=`qwen3-vl`, and a sane pool — never leave production on a broken model.

---

## 6. SET PRODUCTION TO THE GENUINE WINNER

After the bake-off, set `default_extraction_route` (and pool order) and `default_vision_route` to the models that won on **quality first**, then cost/latency — judged on faithful, complete extraction of the diagram (the owner explicitly rejects under-extraction like lite's 7-claims-from-74-edges). State your pick and the evidence. Keep `enforce_model_capabilities=true`. Leave the pool ordered so the best model is primary and the remaining *working* models form a real fallback chain (no broken model may sit in a pool).

---

## 7. COMMIT, PUSH, DOCUMENT

- Stage only the specific changed paths (`git add <paths>`, never `git add -A`). Expected: the three adapters (`openai-adapter.ts`, `google-gemini-adapter.ts`, and possibly `vertex-gemini-adapter.ts`/`cache-utils.ts`), `extraction-system.ts` if you adjusted optional/nullable handling, `catalog.ts`/`candidates.ts`/`types.ts` if you refined capability metadata, the settings write path file(s) for Bug 4, and any `__verify__` tests you added.
- Conventional-commit message(s), e.g. `fix(ai): make all four model-adapter bugs fail-correct (OpenAI strict, Gemini timeout, thinkingLevel, settings encoding)`. Include a `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>` trailer.
- Push to `origin/main` (this repo commits directly to main; the deployed worker must match committed source — do not leave deployed code uncommitted).
- Update `HANDOFF.md` (and `AGENTS.md`/`docs/configuration.md` only if a documented fact changed, e.g. the new env var `GOOGLE_GEMINI_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS` or the chosen production models). Keep the canonical-doc rules in `AGENTS.md`.
- **Do not commit secrets**, `.env.local`, keys, or the bake-off scratch dumps (add scratch files to a tmp dir or delete them; do not stage them).

---

## 8. FINAL REPORT BACK (this is your returned message)

Return a single comprehensive report containing:
1. **Per-bug:** root cause (one sentence), the exact fix (file:line), and the **live-API proof** that it now works (which model, what result).
2. **Bake-off results table:** every extraction + vision candidate × {claims, valid/rejected, cost, latency, quality note}. Name the winner and why.
3. **Production end-state:** the exact `default_extraction_route`, `default_vision_route`, pools, and `enforce_model_capabilities`, read back from prod and confirmed single-encoded.
4. **Deploy:** the worker version/deploy id now live.
5. **Commit:** the SHA(s) pushed to `origin/main` and the files in each.
6. **Anything you could not fully verify** (e.g. a missing key) — stated plainly, not buried.

Do not end the task until production runs a real image ingestion end-to-end (vision → extraction → claims) on the deployed worker with the chosen models and you have confirmed rich, schema-valid, faithful claims. That end-to-end run is the definition of done.

---
name: no-hardcoded-model-names-in-adapters
description: Oracle rule — model-specific facts live in the catalog/capability layer, never hardcoded in provider adapters
metadata:
  type: feedback
---

Albert is explicit and repeatedly insistent: **no model-specific values may be hard-coded in provider adapters** (e.g. no `if (modelId === 'gemini-2.5-flash')`). Model facts (capabilities, how a model expresses thinking, vision support, etc.) live in the catalog/capability layer (`packages/ai/src/routes/catalog.ts`, `candidates.ts`, `model-capabilities/`) and flow through the resolved `OracleModelRoute`.

**Why:** keeps adapters generic and correct as the model roster changes; capability drift is fixed in one metadata place, not scattered across adapters.

**How to apply:** when an adapter must behave differently per model (e.g. `thinkingLevel` enum vs numeric `thinkingBudget`), add a capability field to the route/catalog and branch on that field — not on the model id string.

Also a standing demand: "Proper, long-term fix. Never a band-aid." Don't `.catch()` away validation errors or weaken validation to make something pass. See [[oracle-mandate-adapter-bugs]].

aiTitle: Fix document-ingestion pipeline extraction model failures

content.content:
1	# Test Plan: extraction & vision MODEL BAKE-OFF + pipeline validation
2	
3	Status: **READY TO EXECUTE.** Self-contained for someone with ZERO prior knowledge. Written 2026-06-26.
4	
5	> ⚠️ READ THIS FIRST — INSTRUCTIONS TO THE TESTING AI SESSION ⚠️
6	> This is not a checklist to skim. You MUST actually run every command, capture every number, and produce EVERY deliverable in Part E **in full**. A short summary is a FAILED test. The point is an evidence-backed, side-by-side comparison of real model outputs on real production data, with scores and a defensible recommendation. If a step is blocked, say exactly why and what you tried — do not skip silently. Budget for this taking a while: ~5 extraction runs + ~2 vision runs + analysis. Do not stop early.
7	
8	---
9	
10	## 0. Orientation (you have no prior context — read this)
11	
12	**The Oracle** is an evidence-backed enterprise knowledge graph (`pnpm`+`turbo` TypeScript monorepo). Employees upload documents/images; background jobs turn them into **claims** (operational facts) each backed by a verbatim **quote**.
13	
14	A flowchart **image** is processed in **two passes**:
15	- **Pass 1 — VISION:** a vision model transcribes the image to faithful TEXT (persisted as `document_chunks`). Controlled by setting `default_vision_route`.
16	- **Pass 2 — EXTRACTION:** an extraction model reads that text and emits structured **claims** (JSON validated against a strict Zod schema). Controlled by setting `default_extraction_route`.
17	
18	Repo layout you need: `apps/workers/` = Trigger.dev background jobs (incl. `document-ingestion`); `packages/ai/` = the model router + provider adapters; `packages/db/` = Postgres schema (Supabase). Deploys: workers via Trigger.dev (manual), web via Vercel (push to `main`).
19	
20	**What is already confirmed working (as of 2026-06-26, do NOT re-litigate):**
21	- Vision on `qwen/qwen3-vl-235b-a22b-thinking` works and produces a rich transcription (~11–13k chars, ~84 one-line edges) with **no silent fallback**.
22	- The model-routing refactor is live: no `fallbackRouteId`, failures fail loud, the approved **pool** is the fallback chain, `enforce_model_capabilities=true`, and per-attempt records land in `model_run_attempts`.
23	- The extraction BLOCKER (loose-JSON Qwen → 0 claims) is resolved by using a native-strict-JSON model.
24	
25	**The open question this plan answers:** *which* extraction model, and *which* vision model, give the best quality for the money. The current pick `google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite` is strict-JSON and unblocks extraction but **under-extracts badly** — it returned only **7 claims (1 dependency)** from a transcription containing **84 handoff edges**. We need a real comparison.
26	
27	**The test artifact** is one document: the "Pop Creations Flow" swimlane flowchart, document id **`9d09fa89-3a46-465e-a98b-837287c9e22a`**. It has ~63 boxes across 14 swimlanes (columns = roles/departments: Albert, Buyer, Sales, Creative direction, Junior designer, Technical designers, Creative designers, Sourcing, Production, Carlos, Gina, Licensing Team, Licensor, Factories) and ~80+ directional arrows (handoffs, with conditional branch labels like "If Audit: Fail"). The IMAGE itself is the ground truth (see Appendix C).
28	
29	---
30	
31	## 1. The models under test
32	
33	### Extraction bake-off (3 models — ALL native strict-JSON, which is required for this stage)
34	| Label | Settings model id (CONFIRM exact id — see §3.2) | Provider family | Why it's in the test |
35	|---|---|---|---|
36	| **M1** | `openai/gpt-4o-mini` | OpenAI | cheap baseline |
37	| **M2** | `google/gemini-2.5-flash` | Google Gemini | strict-JSON; earlier produced 56–81 claims on this doc as the old fallback |
38	| **M3** | `openai/gpt-4.1-mini` | OpenAI | newer mini, strict-JSON |
39	
40	(Do NOT test Qwen for extraction — it uses loose tool-call output and malforms the schema; that failure is already documented.)
41	
42	### Vision bake-off (2 models)
43	| Label | Settings model id (CONFIRM) | Why |
44	|---|---|---|
45	| **V1** | `qwen/qwen3-vl-235b-a22b-thinking` | current prod vision; large thinking VL model |
46	| **V2** | `google/gemini-2.5-flash` | the cost-appropriate Gemini *vision* (NOT image-generation) model. Rationale: diagram transcription is OCR + layout perception, not deep reasoning. Gemini **2.5 Flash** is strong at dense OCR/document understanding at low cost; **Gemini 2.5/3 Pro would be overpaying** for functionality we don't need, and **Flash-Lite/2.0-Flash risk dropping small swimlane text**. If you believe a different non-generation Gemini is a better cost/quality fit, you MAY add a third vision model V3 and justify it — but V2 must be `google/gemini-2.5-flash`. NEVER use any `*-image-*` / image-generation model for vision (that was a root-cause bug). |
47	
48	---
49	
50	## 2. Prerequisites & access (verify ALL before testing)
51	
52	1. **Code deployed:** the refactored worker must be live. Confirm the latest Trigger.dev deploy is newer than the routing refactor; if unsure, deploy: `corepack pnpm --filter @oracle/workers run deploy` (the `run` keyword is required). Web: confirm Vercel deployed latest `main`.
53	2. **Migration applied:** `corepack pnpm db:migrate` (idempotent); `corepack pnpm db:check-drift` should be clean. `78_fail_loud_model_routing_settings.sql` must be applied (it adds `enforce_model_capabilities` etc.).
54	3. **Provider keys in prod (Trigger.dev prod env + Vercel):** `OPENAI_API_KEY`, `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS_JSON` (for `google/*`), and for V1 vision `DASHSCOPE_API_KEY` + `DASHSCOPE_BASE_URL=https://dashscope-intl.aliyuncs.com/compatible-mode/v1`. If a key is missing, the model can't run — and with the new refactor it will FAIL LOUD (it will NOT silently use another model). Verify keys exist before blaming a model.
55	4. **Prod DB (read + a few setting writes):** use the **session pooler** string from 1Password item *"Supabase DB Direct URL - The Oracle (CURRENT PROD …)"* → field **`oracle_session_pooler`** (the direct host is IPv6-only and won't resolve from a v4 box). Run query scripts from inside `C:\repos\oracle` so the `postgres` npm package resolves. You may also use the Supabase dashboard SQL editor / Supabase MCP for reads.
56	5. **Trigger.dev** access to trigger `document-ingestion` and read runs (Trigger MCP for project `proj_wgpzsvhmsopqhvwqaycn`, prod env; or the dashboard).
57	6. **Static gates (run first, must all pass):**
58	   ```bash
59	   corepack pnpm -r typecheck
60	   corepack pnpm --filter @oracle/ai run verify:r2
61	   corepack pnpm --filter @oracle/ai run verify:vertex-inline-image
62	   corepack pnpm --filter @oracle/engines run verify:r5
63	   corepack pnpm lint && corepack pnpm build
64	   ```
65	   Record pass/fail. Passing here does NOT prove inference works — that's the runs below.
66	
67	---
68	
69	## 3. Core mechanics you MUST understand before running
70	
71	### 3.1 How to swap the model (no redeploy needed — settings are read at runtime)
72	Two settings rows in the `settings` table drive the passes:
73	- `default_extraction_route` — the extraction model (e.g. `"openai/gpt-4o-mini"`).
74	- `default_vision_route` — the vision model.
75	
76	### 3.2 Capability enforcement is ON — the model must be in the approved POOL and capability-valid
77	`enforce_model_capabilities=true`. The resolver only uses a model for a stage if it is (a) the configured route or a member of that stage's **approved pool**, AND (b) capability-valid. So **before** selecting a model you MUST add it to the pool:
78	- Extraction pool: `model_pool_extraction` (currently `["google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite","qwen/qwen3.7-plus","qwen/qwen3.7-max"]`).
79	- Vision pool: `model_pool_vision` (may or may not be enforced for aux — verify; if vision is single-pick, just set `default_vision_route`).
80	
81	**CONFIRM the exact catalog model ids first.** The picker stores `provider/modelId`. Do NOT guess. Get the real ids from the admin model picker (`/admin/settings`) OR query the catalog:
82	```sql
83	select model_id, provider, supports_structured_output, supports_vision, context_window_tokens,
84	       input_price_per_million, output_price_per_million
85	from model_capabilities
86	where model_id ilike '%gpt-4o-mini%' or model_id ilike '%gpt-4.1-mini%'
87	   or model_id ilike '%gemini-2.5-flash%'
88	order by model_id;
89	```
90	Use the EXACT `provider/model_id` the catalog returns. If a target model is not in `model_capabilities`, it may not be selectable under capability enforcement — note that as a finding and pick the closest catalog id.
91	
92	### 3.3 The run sequence for ONE configuration (memorize — you repeat it per model)
93	For a given `(vision model, extraction model)` config:
94	```bash
95	# 1. Set the pool + the selection (example SQL — adapt model ids):
96	#    UPDATE settings SET value = '["openai/gpt-4o-mini","google/gemini-2.5-flash","openai/gpt-4.1-mini"]'::jsonb WHERE key='model_pool_extraction';
97	#    UPDATE settings SET value = '"openai/gpt-4o-mini"'::jsonb WHERE key='default_extraction_route';
98	#    (and default_vision_route as needed)
99	
100	# 2. Clean + reset the test document, then trigger ingestion:
101	PROD_URL="<oracle_session_pooler>" DOCUMENT_ID="9d09fa89-3a46-465e-a98b-837287c9e22a" APPLY=1 \
102	  node scripts/reevaluate-document.mjs
103	#    then trigger the 'document-ingestion' task with payload {"documentId":"9d09fa89-3a46-465e-a98b-837287c9e22a"}
104	#    (Trigger MCP trigger_task, env=prod, or the dashboard). Wait for the run to COMPLETE.
105	```
106	`reevaluate-document.mjs` deletes the doc's prior claims/chunks/candidates and resets it to `pending_processing`; it aborts safely if any claim is in the Brain/a contradiction/a gap. The trigger then re-runs BOTH passes.
107	
108	### 3.4 CRITICAL fairness caveat for the EXTRACTION bake-off
109	Each full reprocess re-runs vision (Pass 1), and vision is mildly non-deterministic. To compare EXTRACTION models fairly, you MUST **hold vision constant** (`default_vision_route = qwen/qwen3-vl-235b-a22b-thinking` for all three extraction runs) AND **capture each run's transcription stats** (char count + edge count, query in §A). Before comparing extraction outputs, confirm the three transcriptions are comparable (within ~15% on chars and edge count). If one run's transcription is materially different, RE-RUN that extraction config until the input is comparable, or explicitly flag the input difference in your analysis. Do not compare extraction quality across materially different inputs.
110	
111	---
112	
113	## 4. PART A — confirm the pipeline is live (quick smoke, ~10 min)
114	
115	Run once before the bake-off. Record results.
116	1. With current settings, do one reprocess of the test doc (§3.3). Confirm via SQL (§A) the run COMPLETED, vision shows `provider=qwen` with NO fallback, and a `model_run_attempts` row exists for the run. If vision silently ran a different provider, STOP — the refactor/deploy is wrong.
117	2. Confirm the doc reached `complete` (not `failed`, not `complete`-masking-an-error: check `documents.processing_error IS NULL`).
118	
119	If Part A fails, fix deploy/keys before the bake-off — comparing models on a broken pipeline is meaningless.
120	
121	---
122	
123	## 5. PART B — EXTRACTION BAKE-OFF (M1 vs M2 vs M3)
124	
125	Vision is held at **V1 (`qwen/qwen3-vl`)** for all three. Run each extraction model in turn.
126	
127	**For EACH of M1, M2, M3, do this and FILL IN the per-run record (Part E.1):**
128	1. Set `model_pool_extraction` to include all three test ids (once is enough), and set `default_extraction_route` to the model under test (§3.2/§3.3).
129	2. Reprocess + trigger; wait for COMPLETE.
130	3. Collect ALL of the following (SQL in §A):
131	   - which model/provider actually ran extraction (confirm it's the one you selected — NOT a fallback); whether any `model_run_attempts` show an advance.
132	   - the transcription stats for this run (chars, edge count) — for the §3.4 comparability check.
133	   - **claims promoted** (count), **claims by `claim_type`** (esp. how many `dependency`), **candidatesStaged**, **rejections** (from `job_runs.output_json`), and **claims RETURNED by the model** (raw_model_output claim count — to separate under-extraction from rejection).
134	   - input/output **tokens** + **latency** (from `model_runs`) → compute **approx cost** using the catalog pricing (§A) and **cost-per-claim**.
135	   - the **full list of claim summaries + their `claim_type` + the exact `exactQuote`** for this run (you will compare these qualitatively — dump them; do not summarize them away).
136	
137	4. **Quality scoring (score each model 1–5 on each dimension; justify each score in 1–2 sentences with specific examples):**
138	   - **D1 Recall / coverage:** how many of the ~80 real handoffs did it capture as claims? (A model returning 7 claims for 84 edges scores low.)
139	   - **D2 Relationship richness:** fraction of claims that are `dependency`/`exception_rule` (handoffs/branches) vs flat `process_rule` ("box X exists"). Higher = better; the value of a flowchart is the arrows.
140	   - **D3 Correctness of attribution:** spot-check 8 claims against the IMAGE (Appendix C) — do they put each step in the RIGHT swimlane/department and state the handoff direction correctly? Count correct/total.
141	   - **D4 Quote fidelity:** are `exactQuote`s real verbatim spans (they must be — validation enforces it, but check they're meaningful, not trivial 2-word fragments)?
142	   - **D5 Schema conformance & stability:** any rejections / `requiresReview` flags / malformed-field retries? (All three are native-strict-JSON so should be clean; note any surprises.)
143	   - **D6 Cost & latency:** cost-per-run and cost-per-*useful*-claim, plus wall-clock.
144	
145	> Run M2 (Gemini 2.5 Flash) at least **twice** to gauge run-to-run variance (extraction has some nondeterminism). Note the spread.
146	
147	---
148	
149	## 6. PART C — VISION BAKE-OFF (V1 qwen3-vl vs V2 gemini-2.5-flash)
150	
151	Hold extraction constant at the **best extraction model from Part B** (or `google/gemini-2.5-flash` if Part B is inconclusive). Run each vision model in turn (set `default_vision_route`, reprocess, trigger).
152	
153	**For EACH of V1, V2 (and optional V3), collect (SQL §A) and FILL IN Part E.2:**
154	- the FULL transcription text (dump `document_chunks.raw_text` joined) — you will read and compare it.
155	- transcription **char count**, **# of swimlane headers** captured, **# of one-line edges** (`-->` count), **# of distinct box labels**.
156	- vision **tokens** (input = image tokens; output) + **latency** + **approx cost** (§A).
157	- whether vision ran on the selected provider with NO fallback.
158	
159	**Quality scoring (1–5 each, with specific evidence):**
160	- **VD1 Box coverage:** of the ~63 real boxes (Appendix C), how many distinct box labels appear? (count present/missing; list the missing ones.)
161	- **VD2 Lane/role accuracy:** pick 10 boxes; does the transcription place each in the CORRECT swimlane (judge against the IMAGE, column color + position — NOT against the other model's transcription)? Count correct/10. (Note: an earlier session WRONGLY graded Qwen here by comparing to a bad reference — Qwen's lanes were actually correct. Judge ONLY against the image.)
162	- **VD3 Edge/arrow capture:** how many directional handoffs + conditional branch labels ("If Audit: Fail", "Before an Order", etc.) are captured as one-line edges?
163	- **VD4 Fidelity / no hallucination:** any invented boxes/lanes not in the image? (A model that confabulates fails this hard.)
164	- **VD5 Determinism:** run the WINNER twice; how stable is the output (char/edge count, format)?
165	- **VD6 Cost & latency.**
166	
167	**Then run the DOWNSTREAM check:** for each vision model's transcription, note how many claims the (fixed) extraction model produced from it — a better transcription should yield more/better claims. Report vision quality AND its effect on final claims.
168	
169	---
170	
171	## 7. PART D — (optional, destructive) verbose-fail routing tests
172	Only if you have a controlled window (these temporarily break a stage). Record original settings first; restore after.
173	- Set an extraction primary to a model whose provider key is missing, keep a working model later in the pool → confirm it AUTO-ADVANCES to the next approved model and records both attempts in `model_run_attempts` (NOT a silent hidden model).
174	- Make all pool models unusable → confirm `AllCandidatesFailedError` and the document is marked `failed` with that message.
175	- Clear `default_extraction_route` → confirm `NoConfiguredModelError`.
176	- Select a non-vision model for vision → confirm it's refused (capability enforcement), not used.
177	- Confirm any admin fallback-ALERT UI fires (verify whether it was actually built).
178	
179	---
180	
181	## 8. PART E — REQUIRED DELIVERABLES (this IS the test report — produce ALL of it, in full)
182	
183	### E.1 Extraction comparison table (MANDATORY — fill every cell)
184	| Metric | M1 gpt-4o-mini | M2 gemini-2.5-flash (run a / run b) | M3 gpt-4.1-mini |
185	|---|---|---|---|
186	| Transcription chars / edges (input comparability) | | | |
187	| Claims RETURNED by model | | | |
188	| Claims promoted | | | |
189	| of which `dependency` / `exception_rule` | | | |
190	| `process_rule` (flat) | | | |
191	| Rejections | | | |
192	| Input / output tokens | | | |
193	| Latency (s) | | | |
194	| Approx cost / run | | | |
195	| Cost per *useful* (dependency) claim | | | |
196	| D1 Recall (1–5) | | | |
197	| D2 Relationship richness (1–5) | | | |
198	| D3 Attribution correctness (correct/8) | | | |
199	| D4 Quote fidelity (1–5) | | | |
200	| D5 Conformance/stability (1–5) | | | |
201	| D6 Cost/latency (1–5) | | | |
202	| **Weighted total** | | | |
203	
204	Plus, for EACH model: a **2–4 paragraph written analysis** citing SPECIFIC claims it got right/wrong (quote the claim text), where it under/over-extracted, and any failure modes. Include the full claim dumps as an appendix to your report.
205	
206	### E.2 Vision comparison table (MANDATORY)
207	| Metric | V1 qwen3-vl | V2 gemini-2.5-flash | (V3 optional) |
208	|---|---|---|---|
209	| Transcription chars | | | |
210	| Distinct box labels (present/63) | | | |
211	| Missing boxes (list) | | | |
212	| One-line edges captured | | | |
213	| Swimlane headers captured | | | |
214	| VD1 Box coverage (1–5) | | | |
215	| VD2 Lane accuracy (correct/10) | | | |
216	| VD3 Edge capture (1–5) | | | |
217	| VD4 Fidelity / no hallucination (1–5) | | | |
218	| VD5 Determinism (1–5) | | | |
219	| Input/output tokens, latency | | | |
220	| Approx cost / run | | | |
221	| Downstream claims produced (fixed extractor) | | | |
222	| **Weighted total** | | | |
223	
224	Plus a **2–4 paragraph written comparison** with specific examples (e.g. "V2 placed 'Review Audit and send to factory' in Production, but the image shows it in Gina/white — WRONG"), and the two full transcriptions as an appendix.
225	
226	### E.3 Final recommendation (MANDATORY)
227	- **Best extraction model** + 1 paragraph justification grounded in the table (quality vs cost; explicitly weigh "don't overpay").
228	- **Best vision model** + 1 paragraph justification (quality vs cost; explicitly state why the chosen Gemini tier is the right cost/quality point and Pro would be overpaying).
229	- The exact settings to apply the recommendation (`model_pool_extraction`, `default_extraction_route`, `default_vision_route` values).
230	- Any caveats (input variance, run-to-run spread, models not in catalog, keys missing).
231	- Restore prod settings to the recommended config (or to the original if inconclusive) and state what you left them as.
232	
233	### E.4 Pipeline & routing findings (MANDATORY)
234	- Confirm vision/extraction ran the SELECTED models with no silent fallback (cite `model_runs` + `model_run_attempts`).
235	- Any anti-masking signals observed (degraded-doc notes, loud logs, etc.).
236	- Anything that looked wrong but you couldn't fully verify.
237	
238	---
239	
240	## Appendix A — SQL / query library (read-only unless noted)
241	
242	Run query scripts from inside `C:\repos\oracle` (so `postgres` resolves), e.g.:
243	```js
244	// save as packages/db/_q.mjs, run: PROD_URL="<pooler>" node packages/db/_q.mjs ; then delete it
245	import postgres from 'postgres';
246	const sql = postgres(process.env.PROD_URL, { max:1, prepare:false, connect_timeout:25 });
247	const D = '9d09fa89-3a46-465e-a98b-837287c9e22a';
248	// which models ran (most recent):
249	console.log(await sql`select task_type,provider,model,success,error,input_tokens,output_tokens,latency_ms,created_at
250	  from model_runs where task_type in ('document-ingestion','document-ingestion-vision') order by created_at desc limit 6`);
251	// routing attempts (the new table):
252	console.log(await sql`select * from model_run_attempts order by created_at desc limit 10`);
253	// job outcome counts:
254	console.log(await sql`select output_json from job_runs where input_json->>'documentId'=${D} and job_type='document-ingestion' order by started_at desc limit 1`);
255	// claims for the doc, by type:
256	console.log(await sql`select c.claim_type, count(distinct c.id)::int n from claims c
257	  join claim_evidence ce on ce.claim_id=c.id join document_chunks dc on dc.id=ce.source_document_chunk_id
258	  where dc.document_id=${D} group by c.claim_type order by n desc`);
259	// full claim dump (summaries + quotes) for the doc:
260	console.log(await sql`select c.claim_type, c.summary, ce.exact_quote from claims c
261	  join claim_evidence ce on ce.claim_id=c.id join document_chunks dc on dc.id=ce.source_document_chunk_id
262	  where dc.document_id=${D} order by c.created_at`);
263	// transcription text + stats:
264	const t = await sql`select string_agg(raw_text, e'\n') t, count(*)::int chunks, sum(length(raw_text))::int chars from document_chunks where document_id=${D}`;
265	console.log('chunks',t[0].chunks,'chars',t[0].chars,'edges', (t[0].t.match(/-->/g)||[]).length);
266	// claims the model RETURNED (vs promoted): jsonb_array_length(raw_model_output->'claims') per batch
267	console.log(await sql`select eb.status, jsonb_array_length(coalesce(eb.raw_model_output->'claims','[]'::jsonb)) returned
268	  from extraction_batches eb join job_runs jr on jr.id=eb.job_run_id where jr.input_json->>'documentId'=${D}`);
269	// pricing for cost calc:
270	console.log(await sql`select model_id, input_price_per_million, output_price_per_million from model_capabilities
271	  where model_id ilike any(array['%gpt-4o-mini%','%gpt-4.1-mini%','%gemini-2.5-flash%'])`);
272	await sql.end();
273	```
274	Setting writes (use sparingly, record originals first):
275	```sql
276	-- example: select the extraction model under test (ensure it's also in the pool)
277	UPDATE settings SET value='"openai/gpt-4o-mini"'::jsonb WHERE key='default_extraction_route';
278	UPDATE settings SET value='["openai/gpt-4o-mini","google/gemini-2.5-flash","openai/gpt-4.1-mini"]'::jsonb WHERE key='model_pool_extraction';
279	UPDATE settings SET value='"qwen/qwen3-vl-235b-a22b-thinking"'::jsonb WHERE key='default_vision_route';
280	```
281	
282	## Appendix B — command quick reference
283	```bash
284	corepack pnpm -r typecheck
285	corepack pnpm --filter @oracle/ai run verify:r2
286	corepack pnpm --filter @oracle/workers run deploy        # if you needed to redeploy ('run' required)
287	PROD_URL="<oracle_session_pooler>" DOCUMENT_ID="9d09fa89-3a46-465e-a98b-837287c9e22a" APPLY=1 node scripts/reevaluate-document.mjs
288	# then Trigger the 'document-ingestion' task (Trigger MCP / dashboard), env=prod, payload {"documentId":"9d09fa89-..."}, wait for COMPLETE
289	```
290	External IDs: Trigger.dev project `proj_wgpzsvhmsopqhvwqaycn`; Supabase prod `eqccjfbyrywsqkxxpjvg`; test doc `9d09fa89-3a46-465e-a98b-837287c9e22a`.
291	
292	## Appendix C — establishing GROUND TRUTH (do this BEFORE scoring)
293	The IMAGE is the only valid ground truth. Open the original file (it's in Supabase storage bucket `company_documents`; the uploaded image is the "Pop Creations Flow 12112025 (1).png"). Build a reference list ONCE:
294	- Enumerate the 14 swimlane columns (left→right, by header + background color): Albert, Buyer, Sales, Creative direction, Junior designer, Technical designers, Creative designers, Sourcing, Production, Carlos, Gina, Licensing Team, Licensor, Factories.
295	- Enumerate every box and WHICH column it sits in (use the column background color + horizontal position; the columns are color-coded). Known tricky ones a prior session got WRONG by trusting a transcription: "Review Audit and send to factory" is in **Gina** (white column), and "SKUs creation" is in **Carlos** (peach column, 10th) — NOT Sourcing. So verify against the pixels, not a transcription.
296	- Enumerate the arrows (source box → target box, + any branch label).
297	Use THIS list to score VD2 (lane accuracy), D3 (attribution), and box coverage. Do not score against any model's own transcription.
298	
299	## Appendix D — what is already known / out of scope
300	- Qwen is NOT an extraction candidate (loose tool-call JSON malforms the schema → 0 claims). Confirmed; don't re-test it for extraction.
301	- `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` under-extracts (7 claims/1 dependency from 84 edges) — you may include it as a 4th extraction data point for contrast, but the three named models are the focus.
302	- Message-extraction conversation batching (`fix_claim_extr.md`) is implemented but NOT covered here (this plan is the document/image path).
303	- PDF chat attachments are a known gap (no neutral file-part translator).
304
